questions.question: What are Angular components and how do you create one?
questions.answer:
An Angular component is the building block of an Angular application — it controls a piece of the UI (a view) and its associated logic. Every component has: a TypeScript class (logic), an HTML template (view), and optional CSS styles.

// Generate with CLI
// ng generate component portfolio/portfolio-list

@Component({
  selector: 'app-portfolio-list',  // HTML tag used in parent templates
  templateUrl: './portfolio-list.component.html',  // or template: `...`
  styleUrls: ['./portfolio-list.component.scss'],
  standalone: true,  // Angular 17+: no NgModule needed
  imports: [CommonModule, RouterModule]
})
export class PortfolioListComponent implements OnInit
{
  portfolios = signal<Portfolio[]>([]);  // Angular 17 signal
  isLoading = signal(false);

  constructor(private portfolioService: PortfolioService) {}

  ngOnInit(): void {
    this.loadPortfolios();
  }

  private loadPortfolios(): void {
    this.isLoading.set(true);
    this.portfolioService.getAll().subscribe({
      next: data => this.portfolios.set(data),
      error: err => console.error(err),
      complete: () => this.isLoading.set(false)
    });
  }
}

In FolioTrack (Angular 19), I used standalone components exclusively — no NgModules needed, which significantly simplifies the architecture.
followUps: What is the difference between standalone components and NgModule-based components?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between standalon:
This represents the major architectural shift Angular has undergone since v14. Understanding both is important in interviews because most existing codebases still use NgModules.

NgModule-based components (traditional, pre-Angular 14)

  • Every component must be declared in exactly one NgModule
  • Modules declare which components, directives, and pipes they contain
  • To use a component from another module, that module must export it, and you must import that module
  • Results in boilerplate: a separate .module.ts file for every feature area
// Old NgModule approach
@NgModule({
  declarations: [PortfolioListComponent, PortfolioCardComponent],
  imports: [CommonModule, HttpClientModule, RouterModule],
  exports: [PortfolioListComponent]
})
export class PortfolioModule { }

// Must import this module in AppModule to use its components
@NgModule({
  imports: [BrowserModule, PortfolioModule, ...]
})
export class AppModule { }

Standalone components (Angular 14+, default from Angular 17)

  • Components declare their own dependencies directly in the @Component decorator via imports: []
  • No NgModule needed — the component is self-contained
  • Easier to understand, test, and lazy-load individually
  • Bootstrapped with bootstrapApplication() instead of platformBrowserDynamic().bootstrapModule()
// Standalone component — self-contained
@Component({
  selector: 'app-portfolio-list',
  standalone: true,
  imports: [CommonModule, RouterLink, PortfolioCardComponent, AsyncPipe],
  templateUrl: './portfolio-list.component.html'
})
export class PortfolioListComponent { }

// Bootstrap (main.ts)
bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, {
  providers: [
    provideRouter(routes),
    provideHttpClient(withInterceptors([authInterceptor])),
    provideAnimations()
  ]
});

Migration path: Angular provides ng generate @angular/core:standalone schematics to migrate existing NgModule apps. You can mix: standalone components can be used inside NgModule apps and vice versa during migration.

Interview recommendation: For new projects, always use standalone. For existing codebases, understand both — most enterprise Angular apps you encounter will be NgModule-based.
followUpAnswers.What is a component selector?:
A component selector is the CSS selector that Angular uses to identify where to render the component in the DOM. It's defined in the @Component decorator and acts like an HTML tag name (or attribute or class) that you use in parent templates.

// Three types of selectors:

// 1. Element selector (most common) — used as an HTML element tag
@Component({ selector: 'app-portfolio-card', ... })
// Usage: <app-portfolio-card [portfolio]="p" />

// 2. Attribute selector — used as an HTML attribute
@Component({ selector: '[appHighlight]', ... })
// Usage: <div appHighlight>...</div>
// Common for directives, but components can use it too

// 3. Class selector — used as a CSS class (rare, avoid in modern Angular)
@Component({ selector: '.portfolio-card', ... })
// Usage: <div class="portfolio-card">...</div>

Naming conventions:

  • Always use a custom prefix (e.g., app-) to avoid conflicts with native HTML elements and third-party libraries
  • Angular CLI uses the prefix configured in angular.json ("prefix": "app" by default)
  • Never use bare names like card or button — these conflict with or shadow HTML elements

Practical detail: The selector is purely a template-side concern. It has no effect on the component's TypeScript logic, inputs, or outputs. In Angular 17+ with the @let syntax and control flow blocks (@if, @for), you still reference components by their selector in templates.

<!-- Using a component by its element selector -->
@for (portfolio of portfolios(); track portfolio.id) {
  <app-portfolio-card
    [portfolio]="portfolio"
    (favoriteToggled)="onFavorite($event)" />
}

questions.tip: Angular 17+ default is standalone components. NgModules are legacy. New projects should use standalone components. The template: ng new myapp --standalone generates a module-free app.

questions.question: What are Angular lifecycle hooks and when do you use each?
questions.answer:
Lifecycle hooks let you tap into specific moments in a component's life:

  • ngOnChanges — called when @Input() properties change. Receives a SimpleChanges object. First hook called; runs before ngOnInit.
  • ngOnInit — called once after the first ngOnChanges. Initialize data, make HTTP calls, set up subscriptions. Most commonly used hook.
  • ngDoCheck — called on every change detection cycle. Expensive — use sparingly for custom change detection.
  • ngAfterContentInit — called once after ng-content is projected. Access @ContentChild references here.
  • ngAfterViewInit — called once after the component's view (and child views) are fully initialized. Access @ViewChild elements that need DOM rendering here.
  • ngOnDestroy — called just before the component is destroyed. Unsubscribe from Observables, clear timers, release resources. Critical for preventing memory leaks.
export class PortfolioChartComponent
    implements OnInit, AfterViewInit, OnDestroy
{
  @ViewChild('chartCanvas') canvas!: ElementRef;
  private subscription = new Subscription();
  private chart?: Chart;

  ngOnInit(): void {
    this.subscription.add(
      this.dataService.getChartData().subscribe(d => this.updateChart(d)));
  }

  ngAfterViewInit(): void {
    // Canvas is in the DOM now
    this.chart = new Chart(this.canvas.nativeElement, { ... });
  }

  ngOnDestroy(): void {
    this.subscription.unsubscribe();  // prevent memory leak
    this.chart?.destroy();
  }
}
followUps: What happens if you forget to unsubscribe?
What is the takeUntilDestroyed() operator in Angular 16+?

followUpAnswers.What happens if you forget to unsubscrib:
Forgetting to unsubscribe from a long-lived Observable creates a memory leak — the component is destroyed in the DOM, but the subscription (and the closure it holds) remains alive in memory, holding a reference to the component instance and preventing garbage collection.

Consequences:

  • Memory leak: Destroyed component instances accumulate in memory over the app's lifetime, especially bad in SPAs where navigation doesn't refresh the page
  • Ghost event handlers: If the Observable emits (e.g., a timer fires, a WebSocket message arrives), the subscription callback still runs — potentially calling this.someProperty on a destroyed component or triggering multiple UI updates
  • Performance degradation: Dozens of leaked subscriptions each triggering change detection on every emission
// BAD — memory leak if this component is destroyed and re-created
export class PriceFeedComponent implements OnInit {
  price = 0;

  ngOnInit(): void {
    // This interval never stops when the component is destroyed!
    interval(1000).subscribe(n => {
      this.price = n; // runs forever, holding a reference to this component
    });
  }
}

// GOOD — properly cleaned up
export class PriceFeedComponent implements OnInit, OnDestroy {
  price = 0;
  private destroy$ = new Subject<void>();

  ngOnInit(): void {
    interval(1000).pipe(
      takeUntil(this.destroy$)
    ).subscribe(n => this.price = n);
  }

  ngOnDestroy(): void {
    this.destroy$.next();
    this.destroy$.complete();
  }
}

Which Observables NEED unsubscribing:

  • Infinite streams: interval(), timer(), fromEvent(), WebSocket, Store selectors
  • Router events: this.route.params.subscribe()
  • Service Subjects/BehaviorSubjects your component subscribes to

Which DON'T need unsubscribing (they complete automatically):

  • HttpClient calls — complete after the response
  • Observables using take(1) or first()
  • Observables used with the async pipe (Angular handles it)
followUpAnswers.What is the takeUntilDestroyed() operato:
takeUntilDestroyed() is an RxJS operator introduced in Angular 16 (stable in Angular 16, part of @angular/core/rxjs-interop) that automatically completes an Observable when the current injection context (component, directive, pipe, or service) is destroyed.

It replaces the manual Subject + takeUntil + ngOnDestroy pattern with a one-liner.

import { takeUntilDestroyed } from '@angular/core/rxjs-interop';

// Angular 16+: simplest approach — call in constructor or field initializer
@Component({ ... })
export class PriceFeedComponent {
  price = 0;

  constructor(private pricingService: PricingService) {
    // takeUntilDestroyed() captures the DestroyRef automatically
    // when called in the constructor injection context
    this.pricingService.priceStream$.pipe(
      takeUntilDestroyed()
    ).subscribe(p => this.price = p);
  }
}

// If you need it outside the constructor (e.g., in ngOnInit),
// inject DestroyRef explicitly:
@Component({ ... })
export class SearchComponent implements OnInit {
  results = signal<Result[]>([]);
  private destroyRef = inject(DestroyRef);

  ngOnInit(): void {
    this.searchControl.valueChanges.pipe(
      debounceTime(300),
      switchMap(q => this.searchService.search(q)),
      takeUntilDestroyed(this.destroyRef)  // explicit DestroyRef
    ).subscribe(r => this.results.set(r));
  }
}

How it works internally: It uses Angular's DestroyRef (also new in Angular 16) — a lightweight token that provides an onDestroy callback. The operator registers with DestroyRef.onDestroy() and completes the Observable when that fires.

Comparison of approaches:

  • Manual Subscription + unsubscribe(): Verbose, easy to forget, works everywhere
  • Subject + takeUntil(this.destroy$): Clean but requires boilerplate ngOnDestroy
  • async pipe: Best for template-bound Observables, no manual management needed
  • takeUntilDestroyed(): Best for imperative subscriptions in Angular 16+ — minimal code, no boilerplate

In FolioTrack (Angular 19), I used takeUntilDestroyed() for all service subscriptions in components, and the async pipe for template bindings — the combination eliminated all manual unsubscribe code.

questions.tip: takeUntilDestroyed() (Angular 16+) is the cleanest way to auto-unsubscribe: this.dataService.getChartData().pipe(takeUntilDestroyed()).subscribe(...). No manual Subscription management needed.

questions.question: What is RxJS and how do you use it in Angular?
questions.answer:
RxJS (Reactive Extensions for JavaScript) is a library for composing asynchronous operations using Observable streams. Angular uses RxJS heavily — HttpClient returns Observables, the Router exposes Observables, forms have valueChanges Observables.

Key concepts:

  • Observable — a stream that emits values over time. Lazy — nothing happens until you subscribe.
  • Observer — consumes the stream: { next, error, complete }
  • Operators — transform streams: map, filter, switchMap, debounceTime, catchError
  • Subject — both Observable and Observer. Used for inter-component communication.
// Search with debounce — FolioTrack stock search
this.searchControl.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),           // wait 300ms after user stops typing
  distinctUntilChanged(),      // don't fire if value didn't change
  filter(q => q.length >= 2),  // minimum 2 chars
  switchMap(query =>           // cancel previous HTTP call if new one arrives
    this.stockService.search(query).pipe(
      catchError(() => of([]))  // return empty on error, don't break stream
    ))
).subscribe(results => this.searchResults.set(results));

This pattern — debounce + switchMap — is the canonical RxJS autocomplete implementation. I used it in FolioTrack for the stock search feature.
followUps: What is the difference between switchMap, mergeMap, and concatMap?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between switchMap:
All three are higher-order mapping operators — they take each emitted value, pass it to a function that returns an inner Observable, and flatten the result. The difference is in how they handle concurrency: what happens when a new outer value arrives before the previous inner Observable has completed.

OperatorStrategyUse case
switchMapCancel previous inner Observable, switch to new oneSearch/autocomplete, live queries — only the latest matters
mergeMapRun all inner Observables concurrentlyParallel independent operations (fire-and-forget uploads)
concatMapQueue — wait for previous to complete before starting nextSequential operations where order matters (save steps)
exhaustMapIgnore new values while an inner Observable is activeLogin button — ignore extra clicks while request is in flight
// switchMap — cancel previous search when user types more
// If user types 'a', then 'an' before 'a' response arrives,
// the 'a' request is cancelled and only 'an' completes
this.searchInput.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  switchMap(term => this.api.search(term))
).subscribe(results => this.results = results);

// mergeMap — process all file uploads in parallel
from(files).pipe(
  mergeMap(file => this.uploadService.upload(file), 3) // max 3 concurrent
).subscribe(result => this.trackUpload(result));

// concatMap — process payments one at a time, in order
from(paymentQueue).pipe(
  concatMap(payment => this.paymentService.process(payment))
).subscribe(receipt => this.receipts.push(receipt));

// exhaustMap — ignore form submits while one is in flight
this.submitButton.clicks$.pipe(
  exhaustMap(() => this.formService.submit(this.form.value))
).subscribe(response => this.onSuccess(response));

Common bugs from choosing the wrong one:

  • Using mergeMap for search → race condition: responses arrive out of order, UI shows stale results
  • Using switchMap for sequential saves → cancels in-progress saves when user keeps clicking
  • Using concatMap for search → each keystroke queues a request; typing fast results in a long queue of stale requests
followUpAnswers.What is a BehaviorSubject?:
A BehaviorSubject is a special type of Subject from RxJS that has two key properties: it stores the current value and emits it immediately to any new subscriber. It's the most common tool for sharing state between Angular components/services.

Subject vs BehaviorSubject:

  • Subject: No initial value. New subscribers only receive future emissions — they miss anything emitted before they subscribed.
  • BehaviorSubject: Requires an initial value. New subscribers immediately receive the current value, then future emissions. Perfect for state.
// Service using BehaviorSubject for shared state
@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class PortfolioStateService {
  // Private BehaviorSubject — only the service can write
  private selectedPortfolio$ = new BehaviorSubject<Portfolio | null>(null);

  // Public Observable — components can only read
  readonly selectedPortfolio = this.selectedPortfolio$.asObservable();

  // Read current value synchronously (no subscription needed)
  get currentPortfolio(): Portfolio | null {
    return this.selectedPortfolio$.getValue();
  }

  selectPortfolio(portfolio: Portfolio): void {
    this.selectedPortfolio$.next(portfolio);
  }

  clearSelection(): void {
    this.selectedPortfolio$.next(null);
  }
}

// Component A — writes
@Component({ ... })
export class PortfolioListComponent {
  constructor(private state: PortfolioStateService) {}

  onSelect(p: Portfolio): void {
    this.state.selectPortfolio(p);  // notifies all subscribers
  }
}

// Component B — reads (gets current value immediately on subscribe)
@Component({ ... })
export class PortfolioDetailComponent {
  portfolio$ = inject(PortfolioStateService).selectedPortfolio;
}
// Template: {{ (portfolio$ | async)?.name }}

BehaviorSubject vs Signal (Angular 17+):

  • BehaviorSubject integrates with the RxJS ecosystem (operators, pipes, async pipe)
  • Signal is simpler for local component state — no subscriptions, no memory leak risk, synchronous reads
  • Use BehaviorSubject when you need to compose it with other Observables (switchMap, combineLatest). Use Signal for UI-only state within a component or service.

questions.tip: switchMap vs mergeMap vs concatMap: switchMap = cancel previous (HTTP search). mergeMap = run all concurrently (parallel uploads). concatMap = queue them, run one at a time (sequential operations). Wrong choice causes duplicate requests or missed responses.

questions.question: What is Angular's change detection and how does OnPush work?
questions.answer:
Change detection is Angular's mechanism for keeping the view in sync with the component's data. Angular checks all component templates for changes whenever:

  • An event occurs (click, input, timer)
  • An HTTP response arrives
  • A Promise resolves

Default strategy: Angular checks every component in the tree on every change detection cycle. Efficient for small apps; slow for large ones with many components.

OnPush strategy: Angular only re-renders the component when:

  • An @Input() reference changes (not its internal properties — the reference must change)
  • An event originates from the component or its children
  • An async pipe emits a new value
  • You manually call markForCheck()
@Component({
  selector: 'app-portfolio-card',
  changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush,  // opt-in
  template: `<h3>{{ portfolio.name }}</h3>`
})
export class PortfolioCardComponent {
  @Input() portfolio!: Portfolio;  // must pass a NEW object to trigger update
}

// Parent must create new object reference:
this.portfolio = { ...this.portfolio, name: 'New Name' };  // spread = new reference

followUpAnswers.What is Zone.js?:
Zone.js is a library (bundled with Angular) that monkey-patches browser async APIs — setTimeout, setInterval, Promise.then, event listeners, XMLHttpRequest — to notify Angular whenever an asynchronous operation completes. This is how Angular knows when to run change detection without you having to call anything manually.

How it works:

  1. Zone.js wraps every async browser API at app startup
  2. When you click a button or an HTTP response arrives, Zone.js intercepts it and runs the callback inside Angular's zone
  3. When the zone becomes stable (callback finished), Angular automatically triggers change detection across the entire component tree
// You don't write Zone.js code directly — Angular does it for you
// But understanding it explains why this works:

@Component({ template: '{{ count }}' })
export class CounterComponent {
  count = 0;

  increment(): void {
    setTimeout(() => {
      this.count++;
      // Zone.js catches the setTimeout completion and triggers
      // change detection automatically — no manual action needed
    }, 1000);
  }
}

// Without Zone.js (zoneless Angular, available in Angular 18+),
// you must use signals or manual change detection:
@Component({
  template: '{{ count() }}',
  changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush
})
export class CounterComponent {
  count = signal(0);

  increment(): void {
    setTimeout(() => {
      this.count.update(n => n + 1);
      // Signal update automatically marks this component for check
    }, 1000);
  }
}

Zone.js downsides:

  • Overhead: Every async operation (including ones unrelated to UI) triggers a change detection sweep of the whole tree
  • Debugging: Stack traces can be harder to read due to zone wrappers
  • Size: Adds ~100KB to the bundle

Zoneless Angular (Angular 18 experimental, stable in Angular 19+): Angular can now run without Zone.js using Signals for fine-grained reactivity. Only components whose signals changed are checked. This is the future direction — FolioTrack (Angular 19) uses zoneless with signals.
followUpAnswers.What are Angular Signals (Angular 17+)?:
Angular Signals are a reactive primitive introduced in Angular 16 (developer preview) and stable in Angular 17. They provide a way to express reactive state that Angular's change detection can track precisely — knowing exactly which components depend on which state.

Core Signal APIs:

import { signal, computed, effect } from '@angular/core';

// signal() — writable reactive value
const count = signal(0);
count();              // read current value: 0
count.set(5);         // set new value
count.update(n => n + 1);  // update based on current

// computed() — derived value, automatically recalculates
const doubled = computed(() => count() * 2);
// doubled() === 10; recalculates only when count changes

// effect() — side effect that runs when signals it reads change
effect(() => {
  console.log(`Count changed to: ${count()}`);
  // Automatically re-runs whenever count() changes
});

// In a component
@Component({
  template: `
    <p>Count: {{ count() }}</p>
    <p>Doubled: {{ doubled() }}</p>
    <button (click)="increment()">+</button>
  `
})
export class CounterComponent {
  count = signal(0);
  doubled = computed(() => this.count() * 2);

  increment(): void {
    this.count.update(n => n + 1);
  }
}

Why Signals over Zone.js change detection:

  • Precision: Only components that read a changed signal are re-rendered — not the entire tree
  • Synchronous: Signal reads are synchronous; no subscription management needed
  • No memory leaks: No manual unsubscription — signals track dependencies automatically and clean up
  • Interop: toObservable(signal) and toSignal(observable) bridge signals and RxJS
import { toSignal, toObservable } from '@angular/core/rxjs-interop';

// Convert Observable to Signal (great for HTTP calls)
const portfolios = toSignal(
  this.portfolioService.getAll(),
  { initialValue: [] }
);
// Now use portfolios() in templates — no async pipe needed

// Convert Signal to Observable (for use with RxJS operators)
const count$ = toObservable(this.count);
count$.pipe(debounceTime(500)).subscribe(...);

In FolioTrack (Angular 19), I used signals for all component state, toSignal() for HTTP calls, and computed() for derived values like filtered lists and totals. The result: simpler code and no manual subscription management.

questions.tip: Angular Signals (v17+) provide fine-grained reactivity without Zone.js. A signal() knows exactly which components read it and only re-renders those. Much more efficient than the dirty-checking approach. FolioTrack (Angular 19) uses signals throughout.

questions.question: What is the difference between Reactive Forms and Template-driven Forms in Angular?
questions.answer:
Angular provides two approaches to handling forms:

Template-driven Forms:

  • Logic defined in the template using directives (ngModel, ngForm)
  • Simple to set up for basic forms
  • Asynchronous (form model built after view initializes)
  • Hard to test (requires DOM)
<form #form="ngForm" (ngSubmit)="onSubmit(form)">
  <input [(ngModel)]="portfolio.name" name="name" required />
</form>

Reactive Forms (my preference for complex forms):

  • Logic defined in TypeScript, not template
  • Synchronous — form model available immediately in ngOnInit
  • Easier to test (pure TypeScript)
  • Powerful with validators, dynamic form arrays, valueChanges Observable
this.form = this.fb.group({
  name: ['', [Validators.required, Validators.minLength(3)]],
  description: [''],
  holdings: this.fb.array([])  // dynamic array
});

this.form.get('name')!.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300)
).subscribe(val => this.checkNameAvailability(val));

In FolioTrack's portfolio creation form (multi-step, dynamic holding rows), reactive forms were the only viable choice.
followUps: How do you add custom validators to reactive forms?

followUpAnswers.What is FormArray?:
FormArray is a reactive form control that manages an ordered collection of FormControl, FormGroup, or nested FormArray instances. Unlike FormGroup (which has named controls), FormArray is index-based — ideal for dynamic lists where the number of items is not known at build time.

@Component({
  template: `
    <form [formGroup]="form" (ngSubmit)="onSubmit()">
      <input formControlName="portfolioName" />

      <div formArrayName="holdings">
        @for (holding of holdingsArray.controls; track $index) {
          <div [formGroupName]="$index">
            <input formControlName="ticker" placeholder="Ticker" />
            <input formControlName="shares" type="number" />
            <button type="button" (click)="removeHolding($index)">Remove</button>
          </div>
        }
      </div>

      <button type="button" (click)="addHolding()">Add Holding</button>
      <button type="submit">Save</button>
    </form>
  `
})
export class PortfolioFormComponent {
  form = this.fb.group({
    portfolioName: ['', Validators.required],
    holdings: this.fb.array([])
  });

  constructor(private fb: FormBuilder) {}

  // Convenient getter
  get holdingsArray(): FormArray {
    return this.form.get('holdings') as FormArray;
  }

  addHolding(): void {
    const holdingGroup = this.fb.group({
      ticker: ['', [Validators.required, Validators.minLength(1)]],
      shares: [1, [Validators.required, Validators.min(0.001)]]
    });
    this.holdingsArray.push(holdingGroup);
  }

  removeHolding(index: number): void {
    this.holdingsArray.removeAt(index);
  }

  onSubmit(): void {
    if (this.form.valid) {
      console.log(this.form.value);
      // { portfolioName: 'Tech Portfolio', holdings: [{ ticker: 'MSFT', shares: 10 }] }
    }
  }
}

Key FormArray methods: push(control), insert(index, control), removeAt(index), at(index), clear(), length. The value property returns an array of the values of all controls.
followUpAnswers.How do you add custom validators to reac:
Custom validators are plain functions (or classes for async validators) that take an AbstractControl and return either null (valid) or a ValidationErrors object (invalid).

Synchronous custom validator:

// A validator function — reusable across forms
function tickerValidator(control: AbstractControl): ValidationErrors | null {
  const value = control.value as string;
  if (!value) return null;  // let required handle empty

  const isValid = /^[A-Z]{1,5}$/.test(value.toUpperCase());
  return isValid ? null : { invalidTicker: { value } };
}

// Cross-field validator (on a FormGroup)
function passwordMatchValidator(group: AbstractControl): ValidationErrors | null {
  const password = group.get('password')?.value;
  const confirm = group.get('confirmPassword')?.value;
  return password === confirm ? null : { passwordMismatch: true };
}

// Apply to a control or group
this.form = this.fb.group({
  ticker: ['', [Validators.required, tickerValidator]],
  password: ['', Validators.required],
  confirmPassword: ['']
}, { validators: passwordMatchValidator });  // group-level validator

// Display in template
// @if (form.get('ticker')?.errors?.['invalidTicker']) {
//   <span>Ticker must be 1-5 uppercase letters</span>
// }

Async custom validator (e.g., check ticker exists in API):

// AsyncValidatorFn returns Observable<ValidationErrors | null>
function tickerExistsValidator(
  stockService: StockService
): AsyncValidatorFn {
  return (control: AbstractControl): Observable<ValidationErrors | null> => {
    if (!control.value) return of(null);

    return stockService.tickerExists(control.value).pipe(
      debounceTime(400),
      map(exists => exists ? null : { tickerNotFound: true }),
      catchError(() => of(null))  // don't fail validation on network error
    );
  };
}

// Apply as async validator (third argument to control)
this.form = this.fb.group({
  ticker: [
    '',
    [Validators.required, tickerValidator],          // sync validators
    [tickerExistsValidator(this.stockService)]       // async validators
  ]
});

// In template, check pending state
// @if (form.get('ticker')?.pending) {
//   <span>Checking ticker...</span>
// }

Best practice: Put validators in a separate file (e.g., validators/ticker.validator.ts) and use factory functions when they need injected services. This keeps validators testable and reusable.

questions.tip: Reactive forms for anything non-trivial. Template-driven forms become unmanageable when you need conditional validation, dynamic rows, or async validators (check email uniqueness). Reactive forms keep all that logic in testable TypeScript.

questions.question: How does Angular routing work and what is lazy loading?
questions.answer:
Angular's Router maps URL paths to components. Routes are defined in route configuration and loaded by the RouterModule (or provideRouter() in standalone apps).

// app.routes.ts
const routes: Routes = [
  { path: '', component: DashboardComponent },
  {
    path: 'portfolios',
    // Lazy load — PortfolioModule only downloaded when user navigates here
    loadChildren: () =>
      import('./portfolios/portfolios.routes').then(m => m.PORTFOLIO_ROUTES)
  },
  { path: 'login', component: LoginComponent },
  {
    path: 'admin',
    canActivate: [authGuard],  // route guard
    loadChildren: () => import('./admin/admin.routes').then(m => m.ADMIN_ROUTES)
  },
  { path: '**', component: NotFoundComponent }  // wildcard
];

// Bootstrap
provideRouter(routes, withPreloading(PreloadAllModules))

Lazy loading — feature routes are split into separate JS bundles that are only downloaded when the user navigates to that route. Reduces initial bundle size dramatically. Critical for large apps like FolioTrack (Angular 19) where I had 12 feature areas — the main bundle was only 120KB with lazy loading.

followUpAnswers.What are route guards (CanActivate, CanD:
Route guards are functions (Angular 15+) or classes that the router runs before activating or deactivating a route. They return a boolean, a UrlTree (redirect), or an Observable/Promise of either.

CanActivate — prevent navigation TO a route. Most common use: authentication check.

// Functional guard (Angular 15+ preferred approach)
export const authGuard: CanActivateFn = (route, state) => {
  const authService = inject(AuthService);
  const router = inject(Router);

  if (authService.isLoggedIn()) {
    return true;
  }

  // Redirect to login, preserve the attempted URL
  return router.createUrlTree(['/login'], {
    queryParams: { returnUrl: state.url }
  });
};

// Role-based guard
export const adminGuard: CanActivateFn = () => {
  const auth = inject(AuthService);
  return auth.hasRole('Admin')
    ? true
    : inject(Router).createUrlTree(['/unauthorized']);
};

// Apply to routes
const routes: Routes = [
  {
    path: 'dashboard',
    canActivate: [authGuard],
    component: DashboardComponent
  },
  {
    path: 'admin',
    canActivate: [authGuard, adminGuard],  // both must pass
    loadChildren: () => import('./admin/admin.routes').then(m => m.ADMIN_ROUTES)
  }
];

CanDeactivate — prevent navigation AWAY from a route. Classic use: "You have unsaved changes. Leave anyway?"

export interface CanDeactivateComponent {
  canDeactivate(): boolean | Observable<boolean>;
}

export const unsavedChangesGuard: CanDeactivateFn<CanDeactivateComponent> =
  (component) => {
    return component.canDeactivate
      ? component.canDeactivate()
      : true;
  };

// In the component
export class PortfolioEditComponent implements CanDeactivateComponent {
  hasUnsavedChanges = false;

  canDeactivate(): boolean {
    if (this.hasUnsavedChanges) {
      return confirm('You have unsaved changes. Leave anyway?');
    }
    return true;
  }
}

Other guards: CanActivateChild (child routes), CanLoad/CanMatch (prevent lazy module download), Resolve (pre-fetch data before route activates).
followUpAnswers.What is the Router state?:
The Router state is a tree of ActivatedRoute snapshots that represents the current state of the router — which routes are active, their parameters, query params, data, and URL segments.

// Injecting ActivatedRoute gives you the current route's state
@Component({ ... })
export class PortfolioDetailComponent implements OnInit {
  constructor(
    private route: ActivatedRoute,
    private router: Router
  ) {}

  ngOnInit(): void {
    // Route parameters (URL segments like /portfolios/:id)
    // snapshot — current value, won't update if param changes
    const id = this.route.snapshot.paramMap.get('id');

    // Observable — updates if param changes (e.g., /portfolios/1 → /portfolios/2
    // without component recreation)
    this.route.paramMap.pipe(
      map(params => params.get('id')!),
      switchMap(id => this.portfolioService.getById(id))
    ).subscribe(portfolio => this.portfolio = portfolio);

    // Query parameters (?sort=asc&page=2)
    this.route.queryParamMap.subscribe(params => {
      const sort = params.get('sort');
      const page = params.get('page');
    });

    // Static data defined in route config: { data: { title: 'My Portfolio' } }
    const title = this.route.snapshot.data['title'];

    // Parent route data (useful in child routes)
    const parentId = this.route.parent?.snapshot.paramMap.get('id');
  }

  navigateProgrammatically(): void {
    // Navigate with params and query params
    this.router.navigate(['/portfolios', this.portfolioId], {
      queryParams: { tab: 'holdings' },
      queryParamsHandling: 'merge'  // preserve existing query params
    });

    // Access full router state tree
    const state = this.router.routerState;
    const root = state.root;  // root ActivatedRoute
  }
}

Router events — the Router emits events you can subscribe to for navigation lifecycle:

this.router.events.pipe(
  filter(event => event instanceof NavigationStart)
).subscribe(() => this.isLoading = true);

this.router.events.pipe(
  filter(event => event instanceof NavigationEnd
    || event instanceof NavigationCancel
    || event instanceof NavigationError)
).subscribe(() => this.isLoading = false);

Common use: Showing a loading bar during navigation, tracking page views for analytics, or scrolling to the top on navigation.

questions.tip: Route Guards: canActivate = prevent navigation to a route (auth check). canDeactivate = prevent leaving a route ("You have unsaved changes"). In Angular 15+ use functional guards (plain functions) instead of class-based guards.
